analytical_frameworks: Measure-then-claim: capability assertions should rest on published evaluations and benchmark evidence, not demonstrations or marketing
Index-style trend tracking: longitudinal measurement of capability, cost, and adoption curves (the AI Index tradition) to separate real progress from noise
Compute-as-a-strategic-input: tracking who has access to training compute and what that concentration implies for capability diffusion
Policy-capability co-evolution: regulation and capability advance interact; analysis should anticipate the governance response a capability will provoke
Weekly empirical scanning: high-cadence review of new papers and releases, prioritizing reproducible results over announcements (the Import AI method)
recurring_themes: Capabilities routinely arrive earlier than institutions expect, and the gap between frontier and deployed capability is itself a signal
Evaluation infrastructure lags capability; unmeasured capabilities are systematically under-priced by markets and policy
Concentration of compute and talent shapes which actors can act on frontier capabilities
Safety-relevant behaviors and capability jumps should be reported with the same empirical rigor as performance gains
known_cautions: Skeptical of capability claims made without reproducible evaluation evidence
Warns that demonstration-quality results often fail to survive production constraints
Cautions that policy narratives can lag or distort the underlying technical reality
known_biases: Frontier-lab vantage point: closest visibility into the largest labs, which can over-weight frontier developments relative to diffuse open-source progress
Policy-engagement orientation: analysis often frames capabilities by their governance implications
Publication-cadence lens: weekly-scan methods favor developments that surface in public artifacts

sources.title: Import AI newsletter (ongoing weekly analysis of AI research and policy)
AI Index Report (co-founding contributor era, Stanford HAI)
Public policy testimony and essays on AI measurement and governance

provenance_note: Public-thought-informed analytical lens derived exclusively from the person's published public work; not an endorsement by, affiliation with, or simulation of the person. Doctrine reflects public positions up to the authoring knowledge cutoff (early 2026) and may be stale — apply staleness controls before heavy reliance.
forecasting_style: Empirical and measurement-anchored: prefers trend extrapolation from published evaluation data over narrative prediction; flags uncertainty by pointing at missing measurements rather than assigning speculative probabilities.
